title: Insulating between and below rafters in a cold loft conversion
subtitle: Achieving 0.18 W/m2K with PIR boards in a sloping ceiling
excerpt: A loft conversion insulated only between the rafters will not meet current U-value targets and is likely to have condensation problems. The correct approach is insulation between and below the rafters in two layers, with a ventilated counter-batten below for services.
sourceNotes: gov.uk Building Regulations Approved Document L1B (2022) for the 0.18 W/m2K roof target. TRADA guidance on timber-frame insulation. SPAB (Society for the Protection of Ancient Buildings) technical panel notes on insulating traditional roofs. Energy Saving Trust roof insulation guidance.

content.text: A 100 mm rafter filled with mineral wool gives a U-value of about 0.45 W/m2K. Every rafter is left as a
thermal bridge
from the warm room to the cold roof. The right method is two layers: as much wool as will fit between the rafters, then a second layer of
PIR board
below them. The second layer breaks the rafter bridge. It adds more heat resistance without being limited by rafter depth.
What you need to know first
Measure the rafter depth (commonly 100 mm, 125 mm, or 150 mm). Leave a 25 mm clear gap between the top of the insulation and the roof covering for ventilation above the insulation.

Check the existing roof covering. Modern roofs have a breathable felt or membrane. Older roofs are boarded with battens and tiles. Older roofs without a breathable underlay rely on the 25 mm gap above the insulation to clear any moisture in the roof void.

Confirm the room headroom will accommodate the planned below-rafter layer. A 60 mm PIR layer below the rafters plus 12.5 mm plasterboard reduces headroom by 72.5 mm. In a shallow 2.1 m apex room this may be significant.
Worked example: 100 mm rafters, target 0.18 W/m2K

For 100 mm rafters, subtract 25 mm for the ventilation gap: 75 mm usable between the rafters. Using PIR at lambda 0.022 W/mK: 75 mm gives R 3.41. The target total resistance for 0.18 W/m2K is approximately R 5.40, so the below-rafter layer needs R 5.40 - 3.41 - 0.17 (surfaces) = R 1.82. At lambda 0.022: thickness = 1.82 x 0.022 x 1,000 = 40 mm. Use 40 mm PIR below the rafters.

For 150 mm rafters (more common in Victorian terraces with attic conversions): 125 mm usable. R 5.68 between rafters. The below-rafter layer can be reduced to 25 mm PIR. Total U-value with rafter bridging correction: approximately 0.16 W/m2K.
What you need
PIR insulation boards sized to the rafter depth minus 25 mm (cut to width with a utility knife and straight edge)
PIR insulation boards for the below-rafter layer (40 mm or as calculated)
Vapour control layer (VCL): a 500-gauge polythene sheet or specialist VCL membrane
VCL tape for laps and perimeters
25 x 50 mm counter-battens for the service void (optional: allows electrical cables in the void below the VCL)
12.5 mm plasterboard for the final finish
The installation
Clear the rafter bays and check that the underside of the roof covering is dry. Any active leaks must be fixed before insulating.

Cut PIR boards to fit snugly between rafters, leaving a 25 mm gap at the top. Push each board up until it is hard against the roof covering (or the breathable membrane where present). The board should friction-fit without falling.
Tape all joints between boards at the rafter faces with PIR-compatible tape. Seal any gaps at the eaves and ridge with off-cuts.
Fix counter-battens (if using) to the underside of the rafters, 400 mm centres, providing the service void for electrical runs.
Apply the
VCL

as a continuous layer over the counter-battens or directly over the between-rafter insulation. Overlap laps by 100 mm and tape. Turn up 150 mm at all perimeters (eaves, gable walls, ridge) and tape to the adjacent surface.
Apply the below-rafter PIR layer: boards fixed with long screws through the VCL and counter-battens into the rafters. Stagger joints by half a board length in adjacent rows. No gaps at perimeters.
Fix plasterboard to the below-rafter PIR using drywall screws long enough to reach the rafter. Skim, tape, and finish as normal.
Common problems

The VCL must be continuous and sealed at every penetration. An electrical socket box cut through an unsealed VCL allows warm air directly into the insulation. Use back-box liners designed for VCL work.

A common failure is using only between-rafter insulation, leaving the rafters as unbroken bridges from the warm room to the cold covering. The below-rafter layer is not optional if the target is 0.18 W/m2K.
